lesson.title: Introduction to Networking
lesson.objective: Define what a computer network is and explain its purpose
Identify the main types of computer networks (LAN, WAN, MAN)
Describe the key components of a network
lesson.definition: A collection of interconnected devices (computers, servers, printers, etc.) that can communicate and share resources with each other.
lesson.note: The internet is the world's largest computer network, connecting billions of devices across the globe.
lesson.example: A typical home network includes a router, smartphones, laptops, smart TVs, and a printer — all connected and able to share an internet connection.
lesson.definition: A network confined to a small geographic area such as a home, office, or school building. Typically uses Ethernet cables or Wi-Fi.
A network spanning large geographic areas, potentially across countries or continents. The internet is the largest WAN.
A network that covers a city or large campus, larger than a LAN but smaller than a WAN.
lesson.note: Remember the acronyms: LAN = Local, MAN = Metropolitan, WAN = Wide. The key difference is geographic scope.
lesson.codeblock: Network Types by Size: PAN (Personal) — ~10 meters e.g., Bluetooth LAN (Local) — ~1 km e.g., Home/Office WiFi MAN (Metro) — ~50 km e.g., City cable network WAN (Wide) — Global e.g., The Internet

question.text: Which network type typically covers a single building or campus?
The Internet is an example of a WAN.

lesson.exercise: List all the network-connected devices in your home or workplace. Identify which type of network connects them (LAN, WAN, etc.) and how they are connected (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, cellular).

summary.item: A computer network connects devices to share data and resources
Networks are classified by geographic scope: PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN
LANs cover small areas (home/office); WANs span countries/continents
The Internet is the world's largest WAN
